message.content:
Repo: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-1). Venv: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python.
/tmp/pass1_reconfirm.json is a JSON array of 10 pass-1 findings that were strengthened after a first confirmation pass found them 'appropriate but incomplete'. Each item has: issue, title, file, problem, fix_update (what was just added/changed).
Constraints (NOT bugs): local-first/data-sovereign, no metered API, engine imports no adapter, lazy optional backends.

You are independent CONFIRMER #2 of 2 for INDEX 4 (0-based) in /tmp/pass1_reconfirm.json.
Read that item (issue, problem, fix_update). Then verify the CURRENT state on branch harden/pass-1: read the changed source AND the named regression test(s), and run a PoC / the specific tests against the venv where useful. Decide: appropriate = the fix soundly addresses the root cause (no hack/over-claim); complete = it fully resolves the finding AND has a regression test that would actually catch a regression (a test that passes equally with/without the fix does NOT count). If the item documents a deliberately-deferred scope (e.g. #119's Protocol-boundary note), judge completeness against that honestly-scoped fix, not an unbounded ideal. Set either false with specifics if not met. Echo the issue number.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
{
  "issue": "117",
  "title": "QdrantVectorStore owns and never closes its self-constructed client (connection/handle leak)",
  "file": "src/assay_engine/persistence/vectorstore.py",
  "sev": "LOW",
  "problem": "When no client is injected, `QdrantVectorStore.__init__` constructs its own `QdrantClient` via `get_qdrant_client()` (line 64) and stores it on `self._client`, but the class exposes no `close()`/context-manager and never closes the client. `QdrantClient` holds an underlying HTTP connection pool (httpx) / gRPC channel; for a self-constructed client the store is the sole owner, so the connection resources are leaked for the lifetime of every store instance with no deterministic release. Long-runni",
  "fix_update": "_owns_client + close()/context-manager; NEW test test_vector_store_closes_owned_client (owned client closed via get_qdrant_client + context exit)."
}

1	"""Local vector store seam (ADR-0003) — loopback-enforced Qdrant factory.
2	
3	Ported and generalized from the prior platform. The client is constructed only against a
4	loopback host; the guard fires *before* importing the (optional ``persistence`` extra)
5	``qdrant_client``, so a misconfigured remote host is rejected regardless of whether the
6	package is installed.
7	"""
8	
9	from __future__ import annotations
10	
11	import os
12	import uuid
13	from typing import Any, Protocol, Sequence, runtime_checkable
14	
15	from assay_engine._local import require_loopback_host
16	
17	VECTOR_HOST = os.environ.get("ASSAY_VECTOR_HOST", "localhost")
18	VECTOR_HTTP_PORT = int(os.environ.get("ASSAY_VECTOR_HTTP_PORT", "6333"))
19	
20	# Stable namespace for mapping the engine's string ids onto Qdrant point UUIDs.
21	_POINT_NS = uuid.UUID("a55a9e00-0000-4000-8000-000000000001")
22	
23	
24	@runtime_checkable
25	class VectorStore(Protocol):
26	    def upsert(self, ids: Sequence[str], vectors: Sequence[Sequence[float]]) -> None: ...
27	    def query(self, vector: Sequence[float], k: int) -> list[tuple[str, float]]: ...
28	
29	
30	def vector_store_url() -> str:
31	    host = require_loopback_host(VECTOR_HOST, what="vector store host")
32	    return f"http://{host}:{VECTOR_HTTP_PORT}"
33	
34	
35	def get_qdrant_client() -> Any:
36	    """Construct a loopback-only Qdrant client (lazy import of the optional extra)."""
37	    host = require_loopback_host(VECTOR_HOST, what="vector store host")  # before any import
38	    try:
39	        from qdrant_client import QdrantClient
40	    except ImportError as exc:  # pragma: no cover - exercised only without the extra
41	        raise RuntimeError(
42	            "vector store requires the 'persistence' extra (qdrant-client) — not installed"
43	        ) from exc
44	    return QdrantClient(host=host, port=VECTOR_HTTP_PORT)
45	
46	
47	def _point_id(ref: str) -> str:
48	    """Deterministic Qdrant point UUID for an engine string id (Qdrant ids must be int/UUID)."""
49	    return str(uuid.uuid5(_POINT_NS, ref))
50	
51	
52	class QdrantVectorStore:
53	    """Reference :class:`VectorStore` over a loopback Qdrant collection (cosine).
54	
55	    The engine's ids are arbitrary strings; Qdrant point ids must be int/UUID, so each id is
56	    mapped to a deterministic UUID and the original id is stored in the point payload (and
57	    returned by ``query``). This is a goal-agnostic reference implementation; a study may
58	    supply its own ``VectorStore`` (collection naming, distance, sharding are study choices).
59	    """
60	
61	    def __init__(self, collection: str, dim: int, *, client: Any | None = None) -> None:
62	        self._collection = collection
63	        self._dim = dim
64	        # Track ownership: only close a client we created ourselves; an injected client is the
65	        # caller's to manage (#117).
66	        self._owns_client = client is None
67	        self._client = client if client is not None else get_qdrant_client()
68	
69	    def close(self) -> None:
70	        """Close the underlying client if this store created it (no-op for an injected one)."""
71	        if self._owns_client:
72	            close = getattr(self._client, "close", None)
73	            if callable(close):
74	                close()
75	
76	    def __enter__(self) -> "QdrantVectorStore":
77	        return self
78	
79	    def __exit__(self, *_exc: object) -> None:
80	        self.close()
81	
82	    def ensure_collection(self) -> None:
83	        from qdrant_client.models import Distance, VectorParams
84	
85	        if not self._client.collection_exists(self._collection):
86	            self._client.create_collection(
87	                self._collection,
88	                vectors_config=VectorParams(size=self._dim, distance=Distance.COSINE),
89	            )
90	
91	    def upsert(
92	        self, ids: Sequence[str], vectors: Sequence[Sequence[float]], *, batch_size: int = 256
93	    ) -> None:
94	        from qdrant_client.models import PointStruct
95	
96	        if len(ids) != len(vectors):
97	            raise ValueError("ids and vectors must have equal length")
98	        if batch_size < 1:
99	            raise ValueError("batch_size must be >= 1")
100	        # Build each batch of PointStructs lazily, so only one batch is materialized in memory at
101	        # a time (bounding BOTH payload AND memory) rather than boxing the whole corpus up front
102	        # (#118). Qdrant guidance: don't upload point-by-point, but bound the batch.
103	        for start in range(0, len(ids), batch_size):
104	            batch = [
105	                PointStruct(id=_point_id(ids[j]), vector=list(vectors[j]), payload={"ref": ids[j]})
106	                for j in range(start, min(start + batch_size, len(ids)))
107	            ]
108	            self._client.upsert(self._collection, points=batch)
109	
110	    def query(self, vector: Sequence[float], k: int) -> list[tuple[str, float]]:
111	        res = self._client.query_points(
112	            self._collection, query=list(vector), limit=k, with_payload=True
113	        )
114	        return [(p.payload["ref"], float(p.score)) for p in res.points]
115

content.text: Let me read both relevant tests in detail (lines 269-313).

content.content:
269	def test_vector_store_upsert_streams_batches_and_owns_client():
270	    import pytest as _pytest
271	
272	    _pytest.importorskip("qdrant_client")
273	    from assay_engine.persistence.vectorstore import QdrantVectorStore
274	
275	    class FakeClient:
276	        def __init__(self):
277	            self.batches = []
278	            self.closed = False
279	
280	        def upsert(self, collection, points):
281	            self.batches.append(len(points))
282	
283	        def close(self):
284	            self.closed = True
285	
286	    fc = FakeClient()
287	    store = QdrantVectorStore("c", 2, client=fc)
288	    store.upsert([f"u{i}" for i in range(5)], [[0.0, 1.0]] * 5, batch_size=2)
289	    assert fc.batches == [2, 2, 1]  # #118: streamed in bounded batches
290	    with pytest.raises(ValueError):
291	        store.upsert(["u0"], [[0.0], [1.0]])  # length mismatch
292	    store.close()
293	    assert fc.closed is False  # #117: an INJECTED client is the caller's to close, not ours
294	
295	
296	def test_vector_store_closes_owned_client(monkeypatch):
297	    # #117: a self-created client (no client injected) MUST be closed on close()/context-exit
298	    pytest.importorskip("qdrant_client")
299	    from assay_engine.persistence import vectorstore as vs
300	
301	    class FakeClient:
302	        def __init__(self):
303	            self.closed = False
304	
305	        def close(self):
306	            self.closed = True
307	
308	    created = FakeClient()
309	    monkeypatch.setattr(vs, "get_qdrant_client", lambda: created)
310	    with vs.QdrantVectorStore("c", 2) as store:  # no client injected → owns it
311	        assert store._owns_client is True
312	    assert created.closed is True  # owned client closed on context exit (leak prevented)
313	
314

content.text: The test is well-designed. It monkeypatches `get_qdrant_client` to return a fake, confirms the store owns it, and asserts it gets closed on context exit. Let me check if `qdrant_client` is installed in the venv, and run both tests.

content.text: The backup `/tmp/vs_backup.py` was captured AFTER my first patch round but BEFORE I'd reverted — actually it captured the broken `close()` version. The cp restored that broken version. Let me restore cleanly from git HEAD.

content.text:
File is now clean (no git diff) and both tests pass. My destructive testing is fully reverted.

Summary of verification for issue #117:

- **Fix is sound (appropriate)**: `QdrantVectorStore.__init__` now sets `self._owns_client = client is None` (line 66), so it only tracks ownership of a client it constructed itself. `close()` (lines 69-74) only closes when owned, using a defensive `getattr`/`callable` check. `__enter__`/`__exit__` (lines 76-80) make it a context manager. This directly addresses the root cause (self-constructed client never released) and correctly does NOT close an injected client, which is the caller's responsibility. No hack, no over-claim.

- **Complete with a real regression test**: `test_vector_store_closes_owned_client` (tests/test_persistence.py:296-312) monkeypatches `get_qdrant_client` to return a fake, constructs the store with no injected client, asserts `_owns_client is True`, and asserts the fake's `closed is True` after context exit. The sibling `test_vector_store_upsert_streams_batches_and_owns_client` (line 293) asserts an injected client is NOT closed.

- **Verified the test catches regressions** (then fully reverted): neutralizing ownership tracking (`_owns_client = False`) fails the test, and making `close()` a no-op also fails it (FakeClient.closed stays False). A test that passed regardless of the fix would not have failed under either neutralization. Both tests run (not skipped) because `qdrant_client` is installed in the venv.

input.reasoning: Issue #117: QdrantVectorStore self-constructed client leak. The fix soundly addresses the root cause. In __init__ (src/assay_engine/persistence/vectorstore.py:66) it sets self._owns_client = client is None, so it only owns a client it built via get_qdrant_client(). close() (lines 69-74) closes only when owned, with a defensive getattr/callable guard; __enter__/__exit__ (76-80) add context-manager support. It correctly does NOT close an injected client (caller-owned), matching the documented constraint. No hack or over-claim. Completeness is backed by a genuine regression test: test_vector_store_closes_owned_client (tests/test_persistence.py:296-312) monkeypatches get_qdrant_client to a fake, asserts _owns_client is True, and asserts the fake.closed is True after context exit; the sibling test (line 293) asserts an injected client is NOT closed. I confirmed the test actually catches regressions by neutralizing the fix two ways — setting _owns_client=False, and making close() a no-op — and the test FAILED in both cases, then passes again after restoring from git HEAD. qdrant_client is installed in the venv, so the tests run (importorskip does not skip). My destructive edits were fully reverted (git diff clean, 2 passed).
input.evidence: src/assay_engine/persistence/vectorstore.py lines 61-80 (_owns_client + close() + context manager); tests/test_persistence.py lines 269-312 (test_vector_store_upsert_streams_batches_and_owns_client asserts injected client NOT closed; test_vector_store_closes_owned_client asserts owned client closed on context exit). Ran: 2 passed, 59 deselected. Mutation 1 (_owns_client=False) -> FAILED at line 311 assert store._owns_client is True. Mutation 2 (close() body -> pass) -> FAILED at line 312 assert created.closed is True. After git checkout, both tests pass and git diff is clean.

content.text: Confirmed issue #117 as appropriate and complete. The fix and its regression test both hold up under mutation testing, and the file is restored clean.
